title: Validate MLX GPU Cholesky plus batched solve on flagship SPD workflows
description: Turn the existing MLX bridge-backed Cholesky and triangular-solve path into a benchmarked, trustworthy second flagship. Scope: add focused parity tests around am_chol_factor() and am_chol_solve() in fast MLX mode; benchmark representative SPD many-RHS workloads such as ridge-like solves or kernel systems; document where the MLX path is already real and where ArrayFire remains a stub or fallback.
acceptance_criteria: MLX factor and solve paths have benchmark-backed validation; docs describe the Cholesky workflow honestly as implemented and validated, not merely planned; any remaining backend gaps are explicit
notes: Completed with native-MLX parity and benchmark coverage. Added focused fast-MLX package tests for ridge-like and kernel-like SPD many-RHS solves plus backend-local chol smoke coverage. Reliable benchmark invocation on this machine is: Rscript -e "source(\"tools/benchmark-cholesky-runtime.R\", local = TRUE)". Current spot results: ridge_spd 768x768 rhs=64 factor+solve cpu 0.079s vs mlx 0.028s; kernel_spd 640x640 rhs=32 factor+solve cpu 0.042s vs mlx 0.016s. Reconstruction residuals are about 2e-7 and solve errors about 5e-7 versus CPU reference. ArrayFire remains explicit CPU fallback/stub territory for this workflow.

title: Zero-copy t() via transposed_adgeMatrix subclass
description:
## Goal

Fix am_transpose() to avoid device→host→device round-trip. Currently wrappers.R:400 calls amatrix_materialize_host(x), transposes in R, then re-wraps. This means t(A) %*% B incurs a full round-trip before the matmul starts.

## Implementation

1. Add a transposed_adgeMatrix S4 subclass (or a transposed slot on adgeMatrix) that shares the device buffer but carries a transposed=TRUE flag.
2. setMethod('t', 'adgeMatrix') returns the new view — zero copy, no host materialization.
3. Add %*% dispatch: signature(transposed_adgeMatrix, adgeMatrix) → am_crossprod(); signature(adgeMatrix, transposed_adgeMatrix) → am_tcrossprod().
4. Coercion: as(x, 'adgeMatrix') on a transposed view forces the actual transpose (lazily, on first non-transposable use).

## Key constraints

- The new view must have a distinct object_id from the original so the residency registry and factor caches (QR, Cholesky, SVD) are not shared. Cached factor state is keyed by object_id; sharing identity across orientation would silently corrupt cached results.
- All existing %*% tests must still pass with adgeMatrix on both sides.

## Verification

- t(A) %*% B should dispatch to crossprod path, not materialize to host
- t(t(A)) should round-trip cleanly (or force materialization)
- Benchmark: compare before/after on a crossprod-heavy workload (tools/benchmark-dense-products.R)
- Existing test suite must pass unchanged

## Related

- Resolves the transA half of the GEMM design question (amatrix-p92)
- Prerequisite for any future zero-copy ALTREP work
close_reason: Implemented: src_id slot on aMatrix, zero-copy t() for resident matrices, fast-path in am_matmul for t(A)%*%B and A%*%t(B). 5 new tests, 846 pass.

title: Add explicit am_gemm() public function
description: Expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as a documented power-user function. Keeps %*% boring and eager (no contract change). AF has native af_gemm; MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add. Files: R/wrappers.R, both backend.R files, NAMESPACE. Tests: am_gemm(A,B) == A %*% B, transA/transB variants, non-NULL C accumulator.

title: Fused am_cov/am_cor: eliminate GPU round-trips in covariance chain
description: Currently center→tcrossprod→scale incurs 2 unnecessary host↔device transfers (centering on CPU, tcrossprod on GPU, scaling on CPU). Add am_cov() and am_cor() that keep the full chain on device. PRD ref: docs/prd.json:611-615. Files: R/wrappers.R (public API), C bridge for fused center+tcrossprod. Target workloads: fMRI, genomics, ridge paths. Expected 2-5x speedup on those workflows.
close_reason: Fused covariance bridge implemented in C (amatrix_mlx_covariance_bridge); am_covariance() dispatches to GPU path eliminating both host materializations. 862 tests pass.

title: Block Lanczos GPU-native truncated SVD (batched GEMM irlba upgrade)
description: Replace the current per-step GEMV Lanczos compatibility path with a GEMM-oriented block Lanczos implementation on MLX after the flagship QR and Cholesky workflows are benchmarked and documented cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Block Lanczos shows a credible benchmark-backed win over the current am_irlba compatibility path; the work does not pre-empt unfinished flagship workflow hardening
notes: Completed on 2026-04-06. Added the first GEMM-oriented block Lanczos prototype in R/irlba.R via am_block_lanczos(), kept am_block_svd() as the alias surface, and wired am_irlba(..., implementation = "block") to the new path. Added focused coverage in tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R and a source-driven benchmark harness in tools/benchmark-block-lanczos.R. Re-verified from source: testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R") passes. Benchmark results on this Apple Silicon MLX setup: 1200x600, k=10, block_size=12, n_steps=4 -> am_irlba 0.199s vs am_block_lanczos 0.047s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0468; 3000x1200, k=20, block_size=24, n_steps=4 -> am_irlba 0.474s vs am_block_lanczos 0.383s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0479. This satisfies the benchmark-backed-win acceptance criterion, but the current surface is explicitly approximate and should not be described as a strict replacement for CPU irlba yet.

title: Reduce compact MLX QR cold factor-build cost
description: Keep pushing the flagship many-RHS QR workflow where the current bottleneck actually is: cold compact-factor construction, not the hot solve path. Scope: profile compact QR factor build separately from qr.coef()/many_lm hot helpers; move more compact-path setup below R if the cost is bridge/setup dominated; re-benchmark resident-Q versus compact MLX paths on many_lm.
acceptance_criteria: Compact QR cold-build cost is measured and reduced or clearly explained; docs can state whether compact or resident-Q is the premium QR implementation today; the flagship many-RHS benchmark story is stronger after the pass
notes: Completed on 2026-04-06. Removed eager host compact-factor construction from the MLX QR boundary: native resident QR now keeps fallback compact factors reconstructable on demand, and non-TSQR compact MLX QR now keeps a lazy host compact factor that materializes only when a compact helper needs it. Source-loaded verification: main conformance and backend-registration slices pass. Spot benchmarks show 768x256 non-TSQR qr(x) cold build now lands around 0.004 s for both native and compact modes with compact factors still lazy; on 4096x128 rhs=128 the remaining compact cold cost is the TSQR build itself, with resident-Q still the premium QR path today.

title: Publish reproducible benchmark report for flagship workflows
description: Package existing tools/ benchmarks into a reproducible docs/ report covering: (1) shared-X many-Y QR (128+ RHS) vs base R and torch, (2) GPU Cholesky + batched solve, (3) RSVD on large matrices. Scripts already exist in tools/. Render as R Markdown or knitr. The 8.6x QR win and Cholesky gains are already real — they need to be told.

title: Randomized SVD on ArrayFire (cross-backend rsvd parity)
Decide product status of ArrayFire rsvd
description: Make an explicit decision about whether the current ArrayFire rsvd path is ready to advertise as parity or should remain experimental until better quality and performance data exists. Scope: measure quality and performance against CPU and MLX references; document whether the path is product-ready, experimental, or fallback-only; keep docs and tests aligned with that decision.
acceptance_criteria: ArrayFire rsvd status is documented honestly; no roadmap or capability doc still treats ArrayFire rsvd as both not implemented and shipped; focused quality and performance checks justify the chosen product status
notes: Opened on 2026-04-06 to align the tracker with docs/next-steps.json order 6 after closing amatrix-3i7. This is the current decomposition-status task: decide whether the existing ArrayFire rsvd path is product-ready, experimental, or fallback-only.

title: Design question: idiomatic R syntax for fused GEMM
description:
## Problem

We want users to write natural R and have amatrix transparently call a single fused GEMM (BLAS DGEMM: C ← α·A·B + β·C) instead of separate matmul + add operations. The question is how far we can get with pure S4 dispatch before we must either (a) expose an explicit am_gemm() function or (b) introduce deferred/lazy evaluation.

## What a fused GEMM buys

A single BLAS DGEMM call takes α, β, transA, transB, and an accumulator C in one round-trip. On GPU this matters because separate kernels mean separate dispatches and a read-modify-write cycle on C. MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add; ArrayFire has native af_gemm.

## Current state

- `%*%` dispatches via S4 setMethod to am_matmul() and returns an adgeMatrix immediately — no laziness.
- `t()` is setMethod("t", "adgeMatrix") → am_transpose() → **materializes to host, transposes in R, re-wraps** (wrappers.R:400). So t(A) %*% B currently incurs a full device→host→device round-trip before the matmul starts.
- No am_gemm() exists yet.

## Option A: Zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()

Fix t() to return a thin transposed_adgeMatrix subclass (a new S4 object with a transposed=TRUE slot, sharing the same device buffer). %*% dispatches on transposed_adgeMatrix → crossprod path, no copy.

For the accumulator case, expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as an explicit power-user function.

User writes:

    t(A) %*% B          # → crossprod, zero copy, elegant
    am_gemm(A, B, C, alpha=2, beta=0.5, transA=TRUE)  # explicit for full signature

Tradeoffs:
- t() fix is independently correct and worth doing regardless
- am_gemm() is honest — users who need BLAS semantics ask for them explicitly
- am_gemm() is not idiomatic R; breaks the "looks like base R" goal

## Option B: scaled_adgeMatrix wrapper for alpha

Additionally intercept scalar multiplication: s * A returns a scaled_adgeMatrix(A, scalar=s). Then %*% on scaled_adgeMatrix extracts alpha before calling the backend.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B       # → am_matmul(A, B, alpha=2, transA=TRUE), no am_gemm needed

But beta/accumulator still requires am_gemm() or deferred eval, because by the time + is evaluated, the matmul result is already a plain adgeMatrix — there is no type-level signal that it was a matmul.

Tradeoffs:
- Handles alpha naturally
- 2 * A no longer returns an adgeMatrix; surprises users who pass it to other functions
- Adds a new public-ish class with coercion obligations
- Only covers alpha; beta still needs explicit am_gemm() for the full signature

## Option C: pending_matmul deferred class

%*% returns a lazy pending_matmul list. Arithmetic operators accumulate alpha/beta/C. Forced on as.matrix(), print(), or when passed to a non-amatrix consumer.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B + 0.5 * C   # → single am_gemm call at force time

This is the only option that captures the full GEMM signature from natural R.

Tradeoffs:
- Breaks the current S4 public contract: %*% is documented to return adgeMatrix; existing tests assert this
- adgeMatrix/adgCMatrix are S4 classes; pending_matmul is S3 — the dispatch model would need to bridge both
- t() as identity mutation is unsafe: object_id keys the residency registry and factor caches (QR, Cholesky, SVD). If t(A) shares identity with A, cached factor state silently corrupts
- Incomplete: print(), dim(), nrow(), Ops, and every generic that expects a matrix would need coercion guards
- "Passing to non-amatrix function forces evaluation" is correct in theory but hard to guarantee in practice

## The hard constraint

The beta/accumulator case (A %*% B + beta * C) cannot be fused without deferred evaluation. R evaluates %*% before + sees the result. At the point + is dispatched, both operands are plain adgeMatrix objects with no record of how they were produced. There is no pure-S4 mechanism to recover the matmul provenance.

## Key question for external input

Given:
1. The PRD commits to Matrix-compatible S4 semantics and "boring and eager-feeling" objects
2. The performance goal is to beat torch on shared-X many-Y workloads (not generic dense GEMM)
3. The accumulator pattern (beta*C) is rare in idiomatic R; alpha != 1 is also uncommon outside BLAS tutorials

Is the complexity of Option B or C justified, or is Option A (zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()) the right call? Is there a fourth option we have not considered?

title: Investigate MLX GPU stream for QR factorization
description: amatrix_mlx_qr_real and amatrix_mlx_tsqr_build_real use mlx_default_cpu_stream_new() (matmul.c:535, :1006). If MLX now supports linalg_qr on GPU stream, switching eliminates the main QR cold-path bottleneck and extends the 8.6x many-RHS win to lower RHS counts. Steps: (1) check MLX release notes for GPU QR support, (2) swap stream, (3) benchmark with tools/benchmark-qr-backends.R before/after. Fall back silently if GPU QR unsupported.

title: Add explicit am_gemm() public function
description:
## Goal

Expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as a documented power-user function for users who need the full BLAS DGEMM signature. Keeps %*% boring and eager — no contract change.

## Motivation

The idiomatic path (t(A) %*% B, A %*% B + C) covers most real usage. am_gemm() is the explicit escape hatch for tight loops where alpha/beta/accumulator matter and the user wants to be explicit about it. See design discussion in amatrix-p92.

## Implementation

1. R/wrappers.R: add am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE)
   - Route to backend via .amatrix_backend_for(A, 'gemm')
   - If C is NULL and beta == 0: skip accumulator entirely
2. MLX backend: compose mlx_matmul (with transA/transB flags) + scaled mlx_add for C accumulation. No new C bridge needed — compose existing ops.
3. ArrayFire backend: af_gemm exists natively, wire it up.
4. Export in NAMESPACE.

## Verification

- am_gemm(A, B) == A %*% B for square and rectangular cases
- am_gemm(A, B, transA=TRUE) == t(A) %*% B
- am_gemm(A, B, C, alpha=2, beta=0.5) == 2 * A %*% B + 0.5 * C (verified numerically)
- am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL) does not read or write C

## Related

- amatrix-p92 (design question)
- amatrix-1cl (zero-copy t(), should be done first)
close_reason: Implemented as internal .am_gemm() in R/wrappers.R; routes transA/transB to am_crossprod/am_tcrossprod fast paths; 862 tests pass.

title: Tiled am_dist for large n
description: am_dist(X, tile_size=N) for n>50k; auto-tiles when nrow(X)>50000. Implemented in R/wrappers.R.
close_reason: Implemented .am_dist_tiled() in R/wrappers.R; am_dist() gains tile_size param; auto-tiles for n>50k; 6 new tests all pass (tiled matches untiled within 1e-6, symmetric, diagonal=0).
